content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 4, 1990


Steve White
918 W. Lincoln Avenue
Anaheim, CA  92805

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-90-547

Dear Mr. White:

     This is in response to your letter requesting guidance in
connection with the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your request did not
specify a particular governmental decision, we are treating your
letter as a request for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

     As a member of the North County Community College District
Board of Trustees with a spouse who is a part-time teacher in your
district, must you disqualify yourself from participation in deci-
sions regarding faculty salaries and related issues?

CONCLUSION

     You need not disqualify yourself from decisions concerning
faculty salaries, so long as your spouse's circumstances are not
treated differently from those of others in her job classification.
If a decision relates uniquely to your spouse or if a deci-
sion results in the hiring or firing of your spouse then you must
disqualify yourself.


________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. I-90-547
Page 2

FACTS

You are a member of the North County Community College
District Board of Trustees, an appointed position. Your spouse
Rosamund has been a part-time instructor in the district since
prior to your appointment to the board. You are concerned that
Rosamund's teaching position may create a conflict of interest for
you, particularly in regard to participation in decisions relating
to faculty salaries and related issues.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official, such as
a school board member, from making, participating in, or using his
official position to influence any governmental decision in which
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. (Sec-
tion 87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a
decision, and therefore must disqualify himself from participating
in that decision, if the decision would have a reasonably foresee-
able material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally, on the official or a member of his immediate
family, or on:

Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu-
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to the public of-
ficial within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

Section 87103(c).

Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) further provides that a decision
will be considered to have a material financial effect if it "will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets (other than inter-
est in real property), or liabilities of the official or his or
her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at least $250."

Regulation 18702.1(c) limits the foregoing rules:

(c) Notwithstanding subsection (a) an of-
ficial does not have to disqualify himself or
herself from a governmental decision if:

(1) The decision only affects the salary, per
diem, or reimbursement for expenses the official or
his or her spouse receives from a state or local
governmental agency. This subsection does not ap-
ply to decisions to hire, fire, promote, demote, or
discipline an official's spouse, or to set a salary
for an official's spouse which is different from

File No. I-90-547
Page 3

salaries paid to other employees of the spouse's
agency in the same job classification or posi-
tion....

Therefore, a decision to increase the salaries of all
employees in the same classification as that of your spouse would
not create a conflict of interest for you. However, a decision
that would have an effect which would be unique to your wife, such
as a decision regarding an increase in her salary rather than in
the salaries of all instructors in her job classification, would
not be a decision in which you could participate. See Norton
Advice Letter, No. I-89-532 and Plisky Advice Letter, No.
A-86-149.

Please be advised that the above pertains only to the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act. Other statutes, such
as Section 1090, which may have a bearing on your situation are
not within the administrative jurisdiction of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. We suggest that you contact the Attorney
General's office for advice regarding Section 1090 of the Govern-
ment Code.

If you have any questions regarding the above, please do not
hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Susan L. Egbrow
     Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official, such as a school board member, from making, participating in, or using his official position to influence any governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision, and therefore must disqualify himself from participating in that decision, if the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his immediate family, or on:

Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103(c).

Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) further provides that a decision will be considered to have a material financial effect if it "will result in the personal expenses, income, assets (other than interest in real property), or liabilities of the official of his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at least $250."

Regulation 18702.1(c) limits the foregoing rules:

(c) Notwithstanding subsection (a) an official does not have to disqualify himself or herself from a governmental decision if:

(1) The decision only affects the salary, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses the official or his or her spouse receives from a state or local governmental agency. This subsection does not apply to decisions to hire, fire, promote, demote, or discipline an official's spouse, or to set a salary for an official's spouse which is different from

salaries paid to other employees of the spouse's agency in the same job classification or position....

Therefore, a decision to increase the salaries of all employees in the same classification as that of your spouse would not create a conflict of interest for you. However, a decision that would have an effect which would be unique to your wife, such as a decision regarding an increase in her salary rather than in the salaries of all instructors in her job classification, would not be a decision in which you could participate. See Norton Advice Letter, No. I-89-532 and Plisky Advice Letter, No. A-86-149.

Please be advised that the above pertains only to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. Other statutes, such as Section 1090, which may have a bearing on your situation are not within the administrative jurisdiction of the Fair Political Practices Commission. We suggest that you contact the Attorney General's office for advice regarding Section 1090 of the Government Code.
